title: ScrollView Deep Dive
description: Explore advanced ScrollView features: paging, horizontal scrolling, scroll events, and programmatic control.

slides.title: ScrollView Recap 📜
slides.content:
# Review: The Scrollable Container

We've used `ScrollView` before – it's a component that makes its content scrollable. But it has many powerful props we haven't explored.

* **Renders all children at once** – good for small or fixed content.
* **Can scroll vertically or horizontally**.
* **Supports paging** and scroll events.

slides.title: Paging and Horizontal Scrolling 📄
slides.content:
## Paging Enabled

Set `pagingEnabled={true}` to make the scroll view stop at multiples of its own size – perfect for carousels or on boarding screens.

```jsx
<ScrollView pagingEnabled horizontal>
  <View style={{ width: 300, height: 200, backgroundColor: 'red' }} />
  <View style={{ width: 300, height: 200, backgroundColor: 'green' }} />
  <View style={{ width: 300, height: 200, backgroundColor: 'blue' }} />
</ScrollView>
```

Note: You must give each child a width if using horizontal paging, or a height for vertical paging.

slides.title: Scroll Events
slides.codeSnippet:
<ScrollView
  onScroll={(event) => {
    const offsetY = event.nativeEvent.contentOffset.y;
    console.log('Scrolled to:', offsetY);
  }}
  scrollEventThrottle={16} // fire every ~16ms (60fps)
>
  {/* content */}
</ScrollView>

quizData.question: What does `pagingEnabled` do on a ScrollView?

options.text: It makes scrolling snap to multiples of the view's size

quizData.explanation: `pagingEnabled` makes the scroll view stop at page boundaries, like a pager.

slides.title: Programmatic Scrolling 🎮
Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: For `onScroll`, always set `scrollEventThrottle` to control how often the event fires. A value of 16 gives you roughly 60 events per second, which is smooth for most animations.
